claims.claim_text: The opening outage anecdote presents a specific drift incident with emergency CPU-limit changes and hours of wasted debugging, but no real source is identified.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters | opening anecdote
claims.anchor_text: During one outage, the repository said the checkout service had conservative CPU limits, but production was running with much higher limits that had been added during a previous emergency. The team spent hours tuning database pools and retry logic before realizing that the real workload shape did not match the file everyone trusted.
claims.suggested_rewrite: If Git no longer matches production, responders can waste time debugging the wrong bottleneck because the live workload no longer matches the repository they trust.
claims.rationale: This reads like a concrete incident report but provides no attributable real event, so the fake-specific storyline should be collapsed into a general operational lesson.

claims.claim_text: The module pins its behavioral assumptions to Kubernetes 1.35+ across defaults, reconciliation, managed fields, and autoscaling.
claims.anchor_text: This module assumes Kubernetes 1.35+ behavior for server-side defaults, controller reconciliation, managed fields, and autoscaling resources.
claims.suggested_rewrite: This module assumes current Kubernetes behavior for server-side defaults, controller reconciliation, managed fields, and autoscaling resources.
claims.rationale: The bundled version pin is broader than a clean single inline source can directly support, so removing the exact version is safer.

claims.claim_text: When an HPA owns scaling, GitOps comparison should ignore `/spec/replicas` for that workload rather than ignoring the whole Deployment.
claims.span_hint: section: 1 and section: 2 | HPA ownership discussion
claims.rationale: Kubernetes documents that HPA manages a workload's replica count, and Argo CD documents targeted ignore rules for `/spec/replicas`.

claims.claim_text: Runtime state such as `status`, `resourceVersion`, timestamps, and managed field ownership is system-managed and should not be treated as desired configuration in GitOps diffs.
claims.span_hint: section: 1 and section: 2 | runtime metadata discussion
claims.rationale: Kubernetes object and API docs describe desired-state spec versus system-updated status and resourceVersion, Server-Side Apply documents `managedFields`, and Argo CD recommends ignoring status during diffing.

claims.claim_text: The controller-drift paragraph uses specific tool examples for generated fields and child resources without a clean inline source set.
claims.anchor_text: HPAs adjust replica counts, cert-manager writes certificate status and secret data, service meshes inject sidecars or annotations, and operators manage child objects from higher-level custom resources.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Other controllers may adjust replica counts, update status, inject sidecars or annotations, or manage child objects from higher-level custom resources.
claims.rationale: The ownership principle is sound, but the exact mixed vendor/tool examples would require several separate product sources beyond this clean allowlisted pass.

claims.claim_text: Argo CD tracks sync state separately from health state, so an application can be OutOfSync while still having an independent health assessment.
claims.rationale: Argo CD documents health states separately from sync state, and the getting-started example shows sync and health reported as distinct fields.

claims.claim_text: Argo CD exposes an `argocd_app_info` Prometheus metric with labels including `sync_status` and `health_status`.
claims.rationale: The Argo CD metrics reference explicitly documents `argocd_app_info` and its `sync_status` and `health_status` labels.

claims.claim_text: Flux Kustomizations and HelmReleases report reconciliation via status conditions such as `Ready` and `Reconciling`, rather than a single sync-status field.
claims.rationale: Flux documents `Ready` and `Reconciling` style conditions for both Kustomizations and HelmReleases in their status models.

claims.claim_text: For a Flux Kustomization, `.status.lastAttemptedRevision` is the revision attempted and `.status.lastAppliedRevision` is the revision successfully applied.
claims.span_hint: section: 4 | Flux revision paragraph and YAML example
claims.rationale: The Flux Kustomization docs explicitly define both status fields.

claims.claim_text: The exact `gotk_reconcile_condition` metric reference in the Flux alerting paragraph comes from archived Flux monitoring docs rather than the current canonical metrics page.
claims.rationale: The exact metric name is documented in Flux's archived versioned monitoring docs, but not on the current allowlisted canonical page.

claims.claim_text: In Argo CD, `selfHeal` restores tracked resources to Git state, while `prune` deletes live resources that are no longer declared in source.
claims.rationale: Argo CD's automated sync policy docs distinguish automatic self-healing from automatic pruning.

claims.claim_text: In Flux, `spec.prune: true` garbage-collects objects previously applied by a Kustomization when they disappear from the current source revision.
claims.rationale: The Flux Kustomization docs explicitly define prune and garbage-collection behavior for previously applied objects.

claims.claim_text: `Service.spec.clusterIP` is ordinarily assigned by the cluster control plane unless explicitly set, so it is usually treated as runtime state in drift comparison.
claims.span_hint: Hands-On Exercise Part 1 table | Service `checkout-api` row
claims.rationale: Kubernetes documents that Service ClusterIPs can be dynamically allocated by the control plane, making them cluster-owned runtime values unless explicitly set.

further_reading.title: Argo CD Diff Customization
further_reading.why_relevant: Best primary reference for precise ignore rules, manager-owned fields, and status-field handling in drift detection.

further_reading.title: Flux Kustomization
further_reading.why_relevant: Covers reconciliation conditions, revision tracking, inventory, drift correction, and prune behavior in Flux.

further_reading.title: Horizontal Pod Autoscaling
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary source for replica ownership and autoscaling behavior, which is central to distinguishing real drift from delegated scaling.

notes: No claim entries were generated for diagrams, code blocks, quiz prompts, worked-example file contents, or repeated restatements of the same HPA/health/prune facts unless they introduced a new standalone citable claim. The lead anecdote was treated as an unsupported composite story, and the Flux `gotk_reconcile_condition` reference was isolated because the exact metric name is documented only in archived versioned docs.
